hook: A lived-in Victorian house crammed with curiosities, where the owner gives tours in period dress and lets you try on a cage crinoline.
facts: A private house-museum at 72 Richmond Avenue, Auckland Park, furnished throughout with original late-Victorian and Edwardian antiques and bric-a-brac.
Owner-curator Katharine Love lives in the house and personally conducts the tours, which run up to about two hours by appointment.
Visitors can be dressed as a parlour maid and try on a cage crinoline, the structured under-petticoat that held out Victorian skirts.
Tours are by appointment only, roughly 10:00-17:00, and were priced around R100 per adult (R50 concessions).
The house dates to the early 20th century and presents a typical Johannesburg Victorian-era household.
w: A modest Auckland Park house crammed floor to ceiling with Victorian and Edwardian curiosities -- dolls, a dollhouse, etiquette tales and a maze of interleading rooms. The owner, Katharine Love, guides you through in period dress, sometimes a full crinoline, over verandah tea. A lived-in time capsule, run as one woman's obsession.

hook: A 1904 Herbert Baker mansion built for Randlord John Dale Lace and his scandal-courting socialite wife Jose, now a heritage venue for monthly concerts and tours.
facts: Designed by architect Herbert Baker, with construction begun around 1902 and the Dale Laces moving in in 1904.
Built in the Arts and Crafts style, contrasting koppie stone quarried on site with plastered brickwork and wood-panelled interiors.
Commissioned by Randlord John Dale Lace and his wife Jose Dale Lace, a leading socialite of early Johannesburg.
The Dale Laces lost the house after fire damage and bankruptcy; it was later taken over by mining magnate George Albu.
It is a declared Provincial Heritage Site at 21 Rockridge Road, Parktown, and hosts monthly classical-music concerts and guided tours.
legend: Jose Dale Lace was said to have driven a carriage drawn by a team of trained zebras rather than horses.
John Dale Lace is reputed to have said of his marriage, 'Life with Jose is hell, but without her it's worse hell.'
w: A grand 1904 Herbert Baker mansion in Parktown, built for the Dale Laces -- whose lady of the house, Jose, drove a zebra-drawn carriage, trailed high-society scandal, took to the stage, and married the same man twice. 'Life with Jose is hell,' he is said to have written. A declared heritage site; you tour it by arrangement.

hook: Johannesburg's grand 1880s gentlemen's club on Loveday Street, whose Buckland Library holds what is claimed to be the world's largest collection of Punch cartoons.
facts: Founded in 1887 as a modest brick-and-thatch building where Commissioner Street met Loveday Street, near the start of the gold rush.
The present, third clubhouse was designed by architects Leck & Emley around 1902 and completed in 1904.
An earlier two-storey clubhouse built in 1890 was for a time the tallest building in Johannesburg.
The Buckland Library holds in excess of 10,000 volumes including rare Africana, alongside the Edward Jordan library.
The Buckland Library is said to hold the world's largest collection of Punch magazine cartoons.
It remains a private members' club at 33 Loveday Street, Marshalltown, and is a Johannesburg heritage landmark.
w: Johannesburg's gold-rush gentlemen's club, founded in 1887 by Cecil Rhodes and friends, in a grand 1904 building of dark wood, marble and a sweeping stair. Its Buckland Library holds 10,000-odd rare Africana volumes and what's claimed as the world's largest collection of Punch cartoons. The longest bar in Africa once ran here.

hook: An eight-storey CBD warehouse said to be the largest used and rare bookstore in Africa, with around two million items stacked into every corner.
facts: Located at 244 Commissioner Street in central Johannesburg, billed as the largest shop selling rare and used books in Africa.
It holds an estimated two million items spread over roughly eight floors of a single building.
Founded in 1974 by brothers Jonathan and Geoffrey Klass, starting in a shop above a garage before expanding.
Beyond books it stocks maps, prints, engravings, newspapers, photographs and thousands of vinyl records and 78 rpm discs.
Books fill the elevator and every surface; opening hours are roughly Mon-Fri 09:00-17:00 and Sat 10:00-13:00.
w: Said to be Africa's largest second-hand bookshop -- around two million books, records and ephemera packed up eight storeys of an old CBD building in glorious chaos. Two brothers founded it in 1974 and never stopped stacking. You don't browse so much as spelunk. Bibliographic overload, by the floor.

hook: Gandhi's former Johannesburg home, 'The Kraal,' designed by his friend Hermann Kallenbach and now a small museum and guesthouse.
facts: Mahatma Gandhi lived and worked here between 1908 and 1909, when he was developing his philosophy of passive resistance.
The house was designed by the architect Hermann Kallenbach, who shared it with Gandhi, and was nicknamed 'The Kraal'.
Kallenbach's design (c.1907) drew on the form of two rondavels (round huts) built with European methods.
'Satyagraha' means insistence on, or holding firmly to, truth, the basis of Gandhi's non-violent resistance.
Bought in 2009 by French travel company Voyageurs du Monde, restored, and opened to the public as a museum and guesthouse in 2011.
It stands at 15 Pine Road, Orchards, near Norwood, and offers vegetarian meals and wellness services to guests.
w: The modest Orchards house where Gandhi lived in 1908-09 with the architect Hermann Kallenbach, who built it -- and where he sharpened satyagraha, his philosophy of non-violent resistance, during his 21 South African years. Now a museum and a quiet guesthouse. Where a world-changing idea took shape, on a Joburg ridge.

hook: A traditional muti shop near Diagonal Street whose ceiling and walls are a cabinet of curiosities of dried herbs, skins, horns and bones.
facts: A traditional muti (medicine) shop in the CBD, near Diagonal Street, signed above the door as 'The King of Muti, Herbal and Homeopathic Remedies'.
Established in 1956, it stocks more than 1,900 indigenous herbs prescribed by inyangas (traditional healers).
Its ceiling and walls are hung with dried skins, horns, bones, ox hooves and tails, ostrich feet, beads and a desiccated monkey carcass.
A resident sangoma will, for a small fee, 'throw the bones' and offer divination to visitors.
It is also marketed as the KwaZulu Muti Museum of Man and Science; hours are roughly 07:30-17:00 weekdays, 07:30-13:30 Saturdays.
w: A real working muti shop near Diagonal Street -- a cabinet of curiosities supplying traditional healers with dried roots, animal bones, skins and potent ingredients, the ceiling hung with herbs and hides. A resident sangoma works the counter. Going since 1956, a living window onto African medicine -- not a museum at all, despite the name.

hook: A casino and entertainment complex built as a fake ancient Tuscan village, complete with a painted day-to-night 'sky' ceiling and an adjoining bird park.
facts: Opened on 30 November 2001, the complex is designed to replicate an ancient Tuscan village from over 6,000 drawings.
The main casino's ceiling is painted as a fake sky, transitioning from daylight on one side to dusk on the other.
The gaming, retail and leisure complex includes more than 1,700 slot machines and around 70 gaming tables.
The adjoining Montecasino Bird Gardens house roughly 1,000 birds and small animals in landscaped settings.
The Bird Gardens also hold what is claimed to be the world's largest and most diverse private collection of South African cycads, over 750 plants.
It is located at 1 Montecasino Boulevard, Fourways, and reports over nine million visitors annually.
w: A full-scale fake Tuscan village built inside a Fourways casino -- painted skies that shift from day to dusk, crumbling-on-purpose facades, washing strung overhead, even a fake-aged church. It opened in 2001, and a real bird park sits alongside it. Gloriously, knowingly absurd. Italy, indoors and air-conditioned.

hook: A dining table for 22 guests hoisted dozens of metres into the air by crane, run as a regular weekend event on the grounds of St Stithians College.
facts: Guests are strapped into seats around a single table that is suspended high in the air by crane, with chef, waiter and host working from the centre.
The table seats 22 people per session and is lifted to heights advertised at up to about 50-60 metres.
It runs as a recurring event, typically on Saturdays with several lifts a day, offering breakfast, lunch, sunset and dinner sittings.
The fixed venue is the Higher Ground Restaurant on the grounds of St Stithians College, 40 Peter Place, Bryanston/Sandton.
There is a minimum age of around 10 and a minimum height requirement of about 140 cm to ride.
w: A dining table for 22 winched up by crane high above the city, diners strapped in and feet dangling, the skyline spread out below. The global format touches down at a fixed Bryanston venue for its Joburg seatings. Fine dining with vertigo on the side -- not for the faint of stomach. The most precarious restaurant in town.

hook: A 16-hectare inner-city koppie garden, once a no-go zone, reborn with 100+ laser-cut animal sculptures.
facts: Inner-city park and nature reserve in Houghton, established in 1937, comprising 16 hectares (40 acres).
From 2017 artist James Delaney created a series of brightly painted laser-cut animal artworks scattered through the reserve.
Delaney's project began with the 'Owl Forest', a collection of 67 owls contributed for Mandela Day in 2017.

There are now over 100 sculptures including monkeys, a pangolin, ostriches, kudu, klipspringers and duikers; the largest is a roughly 5m-high pink and yellow giraffe on what is now called Giraffe Lawn.
Delaney has named and signposted sections of the park after the artworks installed in each area.
Once notorious as a crime-ridden no-go zone, the reserve was revitalised through a volunteer-led restoration effort spearheaded by Delaney.
w: A 16-hectare koppie garden of indigenous plants and stone paths in the heart of Houghton, long shunned as unsafe and now quietly reborn. Since 2017 the artist James Delaney has dotted its slopes with laser-cut steel animals -- it began with an owl forest for Mandela Day. Rare skyline views, and an urban Eden few tourists find.

hook: Inner-city quartzite ridge that is a Johannesburg heritage site, holding a 500-year-old Iron Age iron-smelting furnace.
facts: Nature reserve and Johannesburg City Heritage Site; the area was proclaimed a nature reserve in 1958 to preserve its indigenous flora and fauna.
In 1963 archaeologist Revil Mason excavated an Iron Age iron-smelting furnace here; carbon dating of charcoal indicated use at various times between roughly 1060 AD and 1580 AD.
A second, more modern Iron Age furnace on the northern slopes dates to the 18th/19th centuries.
Middle Stone Age 'Fauresmith' tools were collected on the koppies, and a Late Stone Age living floor with tiny flaked tools is preserved.
Stone-walled kraal remains on the northern slopes belong to the 'Central Cattle Pattern' tradition.
In 2026 the reserve launched an augmented-reality experience bringing the ~500-year-old smelter to life for visitors.
w: A rugged inner-city ridge that still holds the deep past -- Stone Age tools underfoot and an Iron Age iron-smelting furnace, carbon-dated to medieval times, excavated here in 1963. A protected heritage site, opened for guided walks on controlled Sundays. The veld Johannesburg was built on, with the city skyline beyond.

hook: A 1918 jacaranda-lined pass through Linksfield Ridge with a free panoramic lookout over the northern suburbs.
facts: Munro Drive is a suburban pass through the Linksfield Ridge connecting Upper and Lower Houghton; it was completed in 1918.
It is named after John Munro, a director of Johannesburg Consolidated Investment Company (JCI), which owned the land known as the Houghton Estate.
The free viewpoint offers panoramic views stretching toward Rosebank and Sandton, and is popular for sunset photography.
Surrounding jacaranda trees make it especially popular in spring when they bloom purple.
The drive is recognised as a national heritage area and is marked with a heritage plaque.
w: A jacaranda-lined hairpin drive cut over the Linksfield ridge in 1918, ending at a free lookout over the whole northern city toward the Magaliesberg. Little-known and quiet, it comes into its own at sunset, or when the lights flick on across Joburg. A secret balcony on the city.

hook: Joburg's 'stairway to heaven' — about 210 hand-laid quartzite steps climbing the Westcliff Ridge, built as Depression-era relief work.
facts: A series of stone stairways with roughly 210 steps in three separate major sections, climbing the steep Westcliff Ridge.
Built largely during the Great Depression (1929-1933) as a public works programme under City Engineer E. J. Waugh, who employed unemployed men to build the stairs from hard local quartzite.
The main entrance is on Crescent Drive; the stairs are public rights of way running through the suburb of Westcliff, with additional access via Wicklow Avenue in Parkview.
The first set runs between Crescent Drive and Pallinghurst Road; a second, shorter set runs between Pallinghurst Road and Woolston Road.
The stairs have been honoured with Blue Plaques recognising their heritage status.
They are popular with joggers and dog walkers and become especially busy in spring when the surrounding streets' jacarandas bloom.
legend: Locals nickname the climb the 'stairway to heaven' for the view of Joburg's tree canopy that rewards you at the top.
w: A public stairway of some 210 quartzite steps climbing the leafy Westcliff ridge -- built as Depression-era relief work in the 1930s and known to locals as the stairway to heaven. Morning joggers grind up it; others come at dusk just to sit. Sweat, sweeping views, and a small daily ritual of the suburb.

n: Delta Park & Florence Bloom Bird Sanctuary
hook: Joburg's 104-hectare green lung, home to the oldest bird sanctuary in Gauteng with 250+ recorded species.
facts: Delta Park covers about 104 hectares in the heart of Johannesburg, adjacent to the suburbs of Blairgowrie and Victory Park; the park was established in 1963.
Within the park the Bloom brothers established the Florence Bloom Bird Sanctuary in 1975, making it the oldest bird sanctuary in Gauteng province.
Over 250 different bird species have been identified and recorded in the sanctuary since its establishment.
The park is criss-crossed by many walking and running trails and includes two dams/wetlands at the sanctuary.
Access points include Road No. 3 and Road No. 5 off Rustenburg Road in Victory Park, and the corner of Pitcairn Road and Penelope Avenue in Blairgowrie.
w: A 100-hectare green lung in the northern suburbs, its Florence Bloom sanctuary -- the oldest in Gauteng, opened 1975 -- drawing over 250 bird species to two quiet hide-fringed dams. Walkers, dogs and twitchers share the paths. A well-kept local secret for an hour away from the traffic.

hook: A farm on the city's NW edge whose giant maze switches with the seasons — a living maize maze early in the year, a reed-walled Elemental Maze in winter and spring.
facts: Located at 82 Boland Street (off Beyers Naude Drive) in Honeydew / Zandspruit, on the north-western edge of Johannesburg.
Confirmed operating in 2025-2026, open to the public on weekends and public holidays with no booking required; weekdays are reserved for pre-booked private and school events.
The seasonal Maize Maze is grown from living maize in the early part of the year; in winter and spring it is replaced by the Elemental Maze built from indigenous reed fencing.
The Elemental Maze is centred on five themed gardens representing Earth, Fire, Wind, Water and Thought (Reflection).
Additional attractions include rope mazes, a labyrinth, a climbing wall, a soccer-shooting game, hoopla and a picnic area.
w: A farm on the city's western edge where you can lose yourself in giant living mazes -- a maize maze early in the year, then a reed-walled 'elemental' labyrinth of five gardens by winter. Puzzles and quizzes wait at the turns. Big-sky farm escape, ten minutes past the suburbs. Open weekends, in season.

hook: Limestone caves ~40 km NW of the city that yielded 'Mrs Ples' and 'Little Foot' — among the richest hominid-fossil sites on Earth.
facts: Set of dolomitic limestone caves in Gauteng, about 40 km (25 mi) northwest of Johannesburg, within the Cradle of Humankind UNESCO World Heritage Site.
In 1947 Robert Broom and John Robinson found the near-complete Australopithecus africanus skull nicknamed 'Mrs Ples'.
The near-complete Australopithecus skeleton 'Little Foot' (StW 573) was excavated from the cave's Silberberg Grotto over roughly two decades from the 1990s.
The site has produced a large share of the world's known Australopithecus fossils and is operated as a research and tourism cave by the University of the Witwatersrand.
Guided tours descend through chambers to an underground lake; the surrounding cave system is studied for its hydrogeology.
w: Limestone caves in the Cradle of Humankind where some of the most important hominid fossils ever found came out of the rock -- 'Mrs Ples', and the near-complete skeleton 'Little Foot', millions of years old. Don a hard hat and descend the dripping chambers. Where the deep story of being human was read from the stone.

hook: A grass-covered tumulus mound in the veld that houses the official human-origins museum of the Cradle of Humankind.
facts: Official visitor centre for the Cradle of Humankind World Heritage Site, about 50 km northwest of Johannesburg off the R400 near Sterkfontein.
The building is shaped as a 'Tumulus' — a giant grass-covered earth mound that evokes an ancient burial site from one approach and a futuristic structure from the other.
Exhibits trace human evolution, including an underground boat ride through the elements (water, air, fire, ice) representing the formation of the planet.
'Maropeng' is a Setswana word meaning 'returning to the place of origin'.
The centre sits in the Magaliesberg/Witwatersberg ranges and is paired with the nearby Sterkfontein Caves on the official Cradle tour.
w: A vast grass-covered mound rising from the veld -- the Tumulus, the museum of human origins at the heart of the Cradle of Humankind. Inside, an underground boat ride and immersive halls trace four billion years from the first life to us. 'Maropeng' means returning to the place of origin. The story of everyone, under a hill.

hook: Thirty hectares of landscaped lawns and wetlands in the Cradle, studded with monumental sculpture from artists worldwide.
facts: Open-air sculpture park on the banks of the Blaauwbankspruit River at Kromdraai, within the Cradle of Humankind near Krugersdorp.
NIROX was established in 2006 and the park comprises about 30 hectares of cultivated lawns, arbors, waterways and wetlands.
It hosts more than 50 permanent and long-term installations by artists from across the globe, plus at least one large-scale curated exhibition each year.
The grounds adjoin a private nature reserve and host the annual winter sculpture exhibition and outdoor concerts.
w: A lush landscaped park of lawns, water and indigenous bush near the Cradle, scattered with large-scale sculpture -- 50-odd works to wander among, strange and playful by turns. Springwater feeds its ponds; artists come on residency to make for the grounds. Open on selected days. Art, set loose in the Highveld.

hook: Africa's first cylindrical skyscraper — a 54-storey concrete cylinder in Berea wrapped around a hollow core, long a symbol of inner-city Joburg's rise and fall.
facts: Cylindrical residential tower in the Berea district adjoining Hillbrow, built in 1975 to 173 m (567 ft) over 54-55 storeys.
It was the first cylindrical skyscraper in Africa and remained the tallest residential building in Africa for about 48 years, until overtaken in 2023.
The design — a hollow-core cylinder forming a 'city within a city' — was borrowed from Bertrand Goldberg's Marina City in Chicago; principal architect Mannie Feldman.
During the building's 1980s-90s decline its open central core filled with refuse several storeys deep before later cleanup and renewal.
legend: Urban myth long held the hollow core was filled with rubbish 'five stories high'; in reality refuse did accumulate in the core during its years of decay before it was cleared.
w: Africa's tallest residential tower -- a 54-storey concrete cylinder of 1975, hollow at its core, ringed by 464 flats around a sheer central void. It slid from glamorous address to crime-ridden symbol of inner-city decay, its core once choked with rubble, and has clawed its way back. A Brutalist landmark you can see for miles.

hook: Two decommissioned power-station cooling towers in Soweto, one painted as Africa's largest mural, now an adventure rig for bungee and BASE jumps.
facts: Twin cooling towers of the decommissioned Orlando Power Station in Orlando East, Soweto; the towers were built in 1951 and the station was decommissioned in 1998.
Both towers are painted — one carries a giant mural described as one of the largest in South Africa, the other functions as an advertising surface.
A platform between the two towers is used for bungee and BASE jumping, including a roughly 100 m jump, plus a swing into one tower.
Each tower stands about 33 storeys high and the pair is a prominent Soweto landmark.
w: Two 1951 cooling towers in Soweto, left behind when the power station closed -- now painted top to bottom, one carrying South Africa's largest mural, and rigged for bungee and BASE jumps off the bridge between them. Apartheid-era giants turned adventure park and canvas. You can leap into the gap, or just stand and stare up.

hook: The 1960 Johannesburg Planetarium reborn in 2025 as an 8K full-dome immersive theatre — the largest digital dome in the southern hemisphere.
facts: The original Johannesburg Planetarium opened on 12 October 1960 on the University of the Witwatersrand's campus in Braamfontein, the first full-sized planetarium in Africa.
After a roughly R90 million refurbishment with Anglo American, it reopened to the public on 1 February 2025 as the Wits Anglo American Digital Dome.
The original Zeiss star projector was replaced with ten digital projectors rendering 8K full-dome resolution, plus an upgraded 8.2 sound system.
It is billed as the largest digital dome of its kind in the southern hemisphere and now hosts science, education and cultural programming.
w: The old 1960 Johannesburg Planetarium, reborn in 2025 as the largest digital dome in the southern hemisphere -- an 8K, 360-degree screen for space shows, astronomy and digital art, on the Wits campus. The star projector retired; pixels took over. The whole sky, indoors and in ultra-high definition.

hook: A 1930s concrete tunnel network under the CBD that once conveyed 33,000 mailbags a week between the Jeppe Street Post Office and Park Station.
facts: Built in the mid-1930s to move mail underground between the Jeppe Street (Main) Post Office, the Kazerne distribution hub and Park Station, bypassing surface traffic.
Letters and parcels rode conveyor belts and were ejected via chutes onto trains; roughly 33,000 bags were handled weekly between 3am and 11pm.
The system was closed around 1956 when Park Station was expanded, and the tunnels were largely forgotten until rediscovered in the 2010s.

The coordinate marks the Main Post Office on Jeppe Street (built 1933-35, designed by Francis David Strong / Public Works Department, opened October 1935), the network's CBD anchor and a defensible surface entry point.
Engineer William Pryce-Rosser is credited with designing the underground tunnel system.
w: Beneath the CBD runs a forgotten network of 1930s concrete tunnels -- some three kilometres of them, built to whisk mail between the Jeppe Street Post Office and Park Station, then abandoned around 1956. Rediscovered decades later, they are a haunting subterranean relic, sealed off but dreamt over for reuse. The city's secret underside.

hook: South Africa's first shipping-container shopping village, built on a disused Melville park.
facts: Opened in 2015 on the site of the old Faan Smit Park in Melville, at 75/76 4th Avenue.
Built entirely from shipping containers; the name comes from each unit being roughly 27 square metres.
Conceived by Citiq Group architect/engineer Arthur Blake, inspired by London's Boxpark and Paris's Pompidou Centre.
Cost about R30-32 million, cheaper than a comparable conventional brick-and-mortar build (around R45 million).
Designed as low-cost incubator retail for starter entrepreneurs, with flexible leases from one-week pop-ups up to two years.
Hosts the Joburg Artist Market and a mix of boutiques, kiosks and restaurants.
w: A village of repurposed shipping containers in Melville, stacked into a little mall of indie boutiques, makers and food stalls -- said to be South Africa's first container shopping centre, opened in 2015 on an old park. Cronuts, vintage and creative clutter. The throwaway box, recycled into a high street.

hook: An old industrial site in Lorentzville reborn as an open-studio creative precinct with a First-Sunday market.
facts: Located at 16 Viljoen Street, Lorentzville, on the eastern edge of inner-city Johannesburg.
Houses more than 35 artists and artisans in open studios, including sculptor James Delaney.
Open daily 8am-5pm; the only day all studios and restaurants open together is the First Sunday market.
The First Sunday market (Makers and Growers Market) runs the first Sunday of every month except January, roughly 10:00-16:00.
Combines artist studios, artisan workshops, food vendors and landscaped urban gardens; entrance is free with secure off-street parking.
Also operates as a weddings and corporate-event venue.
w: A former industrial site in Lorentzville, reborn as a green creative hub -- artists' studios, leatherworkers and metalsmiths, vegetable gardens between the brick sheds, and a busy first-Sunday market. William Kentridge keeps a studio here. Craft, coffee and urban farming, where light industry used to grind.

hook: A 1930s light-industrial complex turned leafy precinct of boutiques, cafes and an indie cinema.
facts: Set in a former light-industrial complex dating from the 1930s, on Stanley Avenue near Milpark.
Reopened as a boutique lifestyle precinct in 2003, arranged around interlinked tree-filled courtyards.
Home to The Bioscope, described as Johannesburg's original independent arthouse cinema.
Mix of designer boutiques (e.g. Lucky Fish, Me&B), delis, a chocolatier, coffee roastery, bakery and gelateria.
Restaurants include Salvation Cafe (known for its cheesecake), Bean There coffee, La Pergola and Saint Germain.
OpenStreetMap places 44 Stanley Avenue and The Bioscope at this coordinate.
w: A warren of 1930s industrial buildings off Milpark, knocked through into leafy courtyards of design shops, galleries, cafes and a tiny arthouse cinema. Reopened in 2003, it set the template for Joburg's industrial-reuse precincts -- low-key, shaded, easy to linger in. Old factory brick, gone boutique.

hook: A pan-African market along Rockey Street where the continent's diaspora trades spices, produce and crafts.
facts: Runs along Rockey/Raleigh Street in Yeoville, just north-east of the Johannesburg CBD.
Known locally as the Yeoville, Rockey Street or 'Congolese' Market; it is a hub of Johannesburg's African diaspora.
Sells fresh produce, African spices, oils and traditional breads alongside clothes, crafts, tailoring and cobbling.
Traders come from across sub-Saharan Africa, including West Africans, Zimbabweans, Mozambicans and Malawians.
Linked to Sanza Sandile's Yeoville Dinner Club, which sources market produce for its meals.
OpenStreetMap places the Yeoville stretch of Rockey/Raleigh Street at this coordinate.
w: A pan-African market spilling along Rockey Street in Yeoville, fragrant with the cooking and trade of the whole continent -- Congolese, West African, Ethiopian -- spices, bold street food, crafts and loud community life. Once bohemian, the suburb became immigrant Africa's Joburg crossroads. The continent, in one noisy street.

hook: A Rosebank gallery strip anchoring contemporary African art, home to Africa's oldest commercial gallery.
facts: Contemporary-art precinct in Rosebank centred on Keyes Avenue, near the corner of Jan Smuts and Jellicoe Avenues.
Anchored by Everard Read, opened in 1913 and billed as Africa's oldest commercial art gallery, plus its sister gallery Circa.
The flagship building, The Trumpet, houses TMRW Gallery and Banele Khoza's Bkhz gallery around a central atrium pop-up space.
Hosts Keyes Art Night on the first Thursday of every month, with galleries and eateries open late.
A curated mix of galleries, design studios and artisan eateries; a 'Keyes Art Mile 2.0' phase added wellness, fashion and design tenants.
OpenStreetMap places the Everard Read Gallery at this coordinate on Jellicoe Avenue.
w: A compact strip of cutting-edge galleries along Keyes Avenue in Rosebank, anchored by Everard Read -- founded 1913, the oldest commercial gallery in Africa -- alongside Circa and others. Contemporary African art at intimate range, away from the big institutions. A few hundred metres, dense with art.

hook: A 200-plus-stall Sunday market spread across the rooftop of Rosebank Mall.
facts: Held on the rooftop of Rosebank Mall, 50 Bath Avenue, Rosebank, every Sunday roughly 09:00-16:00.
Has over 200 stalls selling handmade decor, clothing, jewellery, vintage collectibles, African crafts and artisanal food.
Runs themed editions through the year, including Vintage Market, Dress for Less, Christmas and Boot Sale weekends.
Often features live music, and entry is free.
OpenStreetMap places Rosebank Mall at this coordinate on Bath Avenue.
w: A beloved weekly market on the Rosebank Mall rooftop, going every Sunday -- antiques and vintage finds, global street food, crafts and African curios under the open sky. A Joburg fixture for decades, busy and easy. Treasure-hunting, three floors up.

hook: The first building of the Maboneng arts district, surrounded by inner-city murals and a Sunday market.
facts: Arts on Main, on the corner of Berea Road and Fox Street, was the first development of the Maboneng Precinct.
Maboneng was developed from 2008 onwards by Jonathan Liebmann and his company Propertuity; 'Maboneng' is Sesotho for 'place of light'.
The precinct grew to more than 15 redeveloped mixed-use buildings and over 50 street-art pieces.
Early studio tenants included artists William Kentridge and Mikhael Subotzky.
Fox Street is lined with cafes, galleries and murals; nearby works include Nelson Makamo portraits on Van Beek Street.
Market on Main runs at Arts on Main every Sunday (about 10:00-15:00) and on the first Thursday night of most months.
w: The anchor of Maboneng, the regenerated arts district east of the CBD -- old warehouses on Fox Street reborn around 2008 into galleries, studios, rooftop bars and walls of bold murals. The name means 'place of light' in Sotho. Hunt the back-alley street art, then climb for the view. Inner-city decay, turned creative quarter.

hook: A weekend food-and-design market in the old Neighbourgoods space on Braamfontein's Juta Street.
facts: Operates at 73 Juta Street, Braamfontein, in the former Neighbourgoods Market building.
The artisan market runs every Saturday roughly 09:00-18:00, with an afternoon live-music line-up.
Sells food, art, design and fashion in a student-heavy inner-city neighbourhood.
OpenStreetMap labels this 73 Juta Street building (Neighbourgoods Market) at this coordinate.
w: A Saturday market in Braamfontein at 73 Juta Street -- the old Neighbourgoods venue -- packed with vegan and street food, craft stalls and local designers, strung with fairy lights in a made-over space. Young, hip and loud. Where studenty Braam eats and browses on a weekend.

hook: A 1939 Johannesburg original on the bohemian eastern edge, a haunt of artists and Portuguese cooks.
facts: The Troyeville Hotel was first licensed in 1939 and is one of Johannesburg's oldest surviving hotel-restaurants.
It sits at 25 Albertina Sisulu Road, Troyeville, on the bohemian eastern edge of the inner city near Jeppestown and Doornfontein.
Long a gathering place for artists, musicians, political figures and sportspeople, reflecting Troyeville's bohemian history.
Known for Portuguese and Mozambican cooking, including its prawns and baby chicken, plus a rooftop with city views.
Troyeville was founded on a strip of land sold from the original farm Doornfontein on the Witwatersrand.
OpenStreetMap places The Troyeville Hotel at this coordinate on Albertina Sisulu Road.
w: A 1939 hotel on a Troyeville corner east of the CBD, anchor of a faded mining-era suburb of early-20th-century facades and street art. Long a bohemian institution, its restaurant and bar hold on as the neighbourhood shifts from gold-rush grit to creative reuse. A survivor, in a quarter layered with Joburg's whole story.
